10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102515
For representing ability, the set-theoretic framework of knowledge structure theory (KST;  Doignon & Falmagne, 1985, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ) offers various features that are highly desirable for any test theory. First, it establishes a discrete, non-numerical representation, and dispenses with the a priori not plausible continuity assumption for representing the relevant psychological constructs. Second, the theory is multidimensional, and the dimensionality of the representing structure need not be fixed in advance. Third, the approach avoids aggregating responses over items (as in forming a sum score), and thus can capitalize on the full information contained in the response pattern observed for an individual. By drawing a detailed picture of the individual strengths and weaknesses, knowledge structures provide a theoretical basis for intelligent tutoring systems ( Falmagne, Albert, Doble, Eppstein, & Hu, 2013 ). They allow for designing procedures for adaptive knowledge assessment and personalized learning. KST is formulated for dichotomous items, the responses to which are classified in a binary way as either completely correct, or incorrect. However, applying the approach to a wider class of problems is highly desirable. For example, besides giving full credit to a solution, one might want to grant some kind of partial credit. To be able to classify the responses into more than two categories KST need to be generalized to polytomous items.

The first attempt in this direction is by  Schrepp (1997) , who confined consideration to linearly ordered response categories. Recently,  Stefanutti, Anselmi, de Chiusole, and Spoto (2020)  extended the approach to response categories forming a (complete) lattice, and for the first time provided a characterization of the resulting polytomous knowledge structures. Although this is an important contribution, as far as so-called quasi-ordinal polytomous knowledge spaces are concerned, it still confines consideration to the particular order representing a precedence relation (or, prerequisite relation) on the items as introduced by  Schrepp (1997) . Ordering items in an all-or-none fashion, and thereby lumping together all the response categories, actually does not take into account the polytomous nature of the items. This makes the approach too restrictive from both a theoretical and an empirical point of view. The subsequently developed approach allows for considering a larger class of precedence relations capturing the mutual relationships between the various possible responses that may be given to the items. It is also based on the comparatively weak assumption of polytomous items having partially ordered response values forming a (complete) lattice, but is able to represent existing structure on a more fine-grained level. The development proceeds in various steps. It first extends the set-theoretical framework in a way that allows for translating the situation into one dealing with a set of (virtual) dichotomous items. Then, after applying well-established KST methods, the results are finally reinterpreted in terms of the polytomous items. This advances the current state of the art in various respects. First, it provides a generalized theory of polytomous quasi-ordinal knowledge spaces, which goes beyond the approaches of  Schrepp (1997)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2020) . But notice that these approaches allow for an infinite set of response categories, and an infinite set of items in the latter case, whereas the current development confines consideration to finite sets. Second, it allows for item-specific response scales, i.e., the response values and/or their lattice structure need not be identical across items. Application of this generalized theory is illustrated for data from the 2011 Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study (TIMSS;  Foy, Arora, & Stanco, 2013 ).

Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  define a  knowledge structure  as a pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       in which  
                         Q 
                       is a non-empty set (assumed to be finite throughout the paper), and  
                         K 
                       is a family of subsets of  
                         Q 
                      , containing at least  
                         Q 
                       and the empty set  
                         0̸ 
                      . Here, the empty set  
                         0̸ 
                       refers to the naïve knowledge state, and  
                         Q 
                       refers to the state of complete mastery. The set  
                         Q 
                       is called the  domain  of the knowledge structure. Its elements are referred to as  items  and the subsets in the family  
                         K 
                       are labelled  (knowledge) states . A knowledge state represents the subset of items in the considered domain that an individual masters. More specific structural assumptions on the collection of knowledge states have received particular attention, among them the  knowledge spaces  (closed under union), and the  quasi-ordinal knowledge spaces  (closed under union and intersection).

KST conceives items as being dichotomous. It categorizes the responses into the dichotomy of ‘mastering’ vs. ‘not mastering’ an item (or, ‘solving’ vs. ‘not solving’, respectively). In this paper, the binary case is generalized to a finite set of response values, which may be identified with different types, or degrees of mastering.

Let  
                         V 
                       denote a non-empty set of response values  
                         
                            v 
                            , 
                            w 
                            , 
                            … 
                         
                      , which is assumed to be finite throughout the paper. Then each  (polytomous) state  is formally a mapping  
                         
                            K 
                            : 
                            Q 
                            → 
                            V 
                         
                      . Let  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       denote the collection of all possible polytomous states, i.e., the set of all mappings  
                         
                            Q 
                            → 
                            V 
                         
                      . Alternatively, a polytomous state  
                         K 
                       may be considered as a particular subset of  
                         
                            Q 
                            × 
                            V 
                         
                      . Denoting the pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            p 
                            , 
                            v 
                            ) 
                         
                       in  
                         
                            Q 
                            × 
                            V 
                         
                       by  
                         
                            
                               p 
                            
                            
                               v 
                            
                         
                      , then  
                         
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  v 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                       expresses the fact that  
                         
                            K 
                            
                               ( 
                               p 
                               ) 
                            
                            = 
                            v 
                         
                      . As still another option, when fixing a particular order of the items  
                         
                            p 
                            , 
                            q 
                            , 
                            … 
                         
                       in a given domain  
                         Q 
                       (which will always be the alphabetical order) with  
                         
                            
                               | 
                               Q 
                               | 
                            
                            = 
                            n 
                         
                      , one can identify  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       with the set  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       of  
                         n 
                      -vectors with components in  
                         V 
                      . In the sequel we will switch back and forth between these alternatives, always choosing the most convenient of these notations. While the subset notation is driving the theoretical derivations, the vector notation is preferred in graphical illustrations.

Exactly the same conventions are used for characterizing the manifest behaviour of an individual, to which we refer as a  response pattern , often denoted by  
                         R 
                      . The observable response patterns are to be distinguished from the polytomous states, which represent underlying latent constructs.

Given the (finite) set of response values  
                         V 
                       and a partial order  
                         ≤ 
                       on  
                         V 
                       (i.e., a reflexive, transitive, and antisymmetric binary relation), the relational structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is called a  response scale . The basic assumption in what follows is that  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a lattice. This means that for all pairs of elements in  
                         V 
                       there exist a supremum (least upper bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ) and an infimum (greatest lower bound w.r.t.  
                         ≤ 
                      ). Using an algebraic notation, suprema and infima are denoted by the binary operations join  
                         ∨ 
                       and meet  
                         ∧ 
                      , i.e.,  
                         
                            v 
                            ∨ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            sup 
                            
                               { 
                               v 
                               , 
                               w 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            v 
                            ∧ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            inf 
                            
                               { 
                               v 
                               , 
                               w 
                               } 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            v 
                            , 
                            w 
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      , and we have  
                         
                            v 
                            ∨ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            w 
                         
                       if and only if  
                         
                            v 
                            ∧ 
                            w 
                            = 
                            v 
                         
                       if and only if  
                         
                            v 
                            ≤ 
                            w 
                         
                       ( Davey & Priestley, 1990 ). Via these equivalences the notation  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ∨ 
                            , 
                            ∧ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is commonly used to refer to the lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , encompassing an algebraic perspective on lattices.

In any finite lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       there exist a greatest element (or,  top element )  
                         
                            ⊤ 
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      , such that  
                         
                            v 
                            ≤ 
                            ⊤ 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            v 
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      , and a least element (or,  bottom element )  
                         
                            ⊥ 
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      , such that  
                         
                            ⊥ 
                            ≤ 
                            v 
                         
                       for all  
                         
                            v 
                            ∈ 
                            V 
                         
                      . Moreover, a finite lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is also a  complete lattice , where joins and meets exist for any collections of elements of  
                         V 
                       (e.g.,  Davey & Priestley, 1990 ). For  
                         
                            W 
                            ⊆ 
                            V 
                         
                       the join of all elements in  
                         W 
                       is denoted by  
                         
                            ⋁ 
                            W 
                         
                      , and the meet of all elements in  
                         W 
                       is denoted by  
                         
                            ⋀ 
                            W 
                         
                      .

Schrepp (1997)  also generalized the dichotomous case, but confined consideration to the special situation where  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      
 forms a linear order, which is a complete (or, connected) partial order. This means that all the values in  
                         V 
                       are comparable with respect to  
                         ≤ 
                      . They may be interpreted as representing a gradation of degrees to which an item can be solved. This generalization allows for granting partial credits to proximal solutions which, for instance, is highly useful for evaluating solutions to math problems. Notice that a finite linear order is in fact a particular lattice, for which the joins (i.e., the suprema) coincide with the maxima, and the meets (i.e., the infima) coincide with the minima. The below considered math problems from TIMSS 2011 ( Foy et al., 2013 ) show that a generalization to arbitrary (complete) lattices is desirable.  Stefanutti et al. (2020)  implemented this generalization within the approach of  Schrepp (1997) , which is again generalized by the theory developed below.  Example 2  provides first examples of an arbitrary lattice and of a linear order on a set of response values.

Example 2 
                         Let  
                               
                                  V 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     0 
                                     , 
                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     2 
                                     , 
                                     3 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            .  Fig. 1 (a) shows the line diagram of a linear order  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             on  
                               V 
                            , which forms a chain. In this particular case,  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             coincides with the numerical relation ‘less than or equal’ on  
                               V 
                            . Here,  
                               0 
                             may code for an incorrect response, and  
                               3 
                             for a fully correct response. The values  
                               1 
                             and  
                               2 
                             may be conceived as partial credits, representing increasing degrees of correctness. For the joins and meets of the lattice

                                  V 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≤ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  )

                                  1 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∨ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  2 
                                  = 
                                  max

                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     2 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  2

                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  2 
                                  = 
                                  min

                                     1

                                     2 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  1

                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             on  
                               V 
                             be defined by the line diagram in  Fig. 1 (b) The values  
                               1

                               2

                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            . They may represent different components of the response (e.g., see the TIMSS problems below) the correctness of which can be evaluated independent from each other. Nontrivial joins and meets of the lattice

                                  V

                                  1 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∧

                                  0 
                               
                            .

The structure on  
                         V 
                       gives rise to a structure on any non-empty collection  
                         
                            K 
                            ⊆ 
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                       of polytomous states. Given a lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , for all  
                         
                            K 
                            , 
                            L 
                            ∈ 
                            K 
                         
                       we can define a partial order  
                         ⊑ 
                       on  
                         K 
                       in an item-wise manner by  
                         
                            (1) 
                            
                               
                                  K 
                                  ⊑ 
                                  L 
                                  iff 
                                  
                                  K 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     p 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ≤ 
                                  L 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     p 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                  for all 
                                  
                                  p 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                                  . 
                               
                            
                         
                      Whenever suprema and infima of pairs of elements of  
                         K 
                       with respect to  
                         ⊑ 
                       exist, let

                       and  
                         ⊓

                         K 
                      . Moreover, for any collection  
                         
                            M 
                            ⊆ 
                            K 
                         
                      , given they exist,  
                         
                            ⨆ 
                            M 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ⨅ 
                            M 
                         
                       denote the join and the meet of all elements in  
                         M 
                      , respectively.

Definition 1 
                         Let  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  , 
                                  V 
                               
                             be finite non-empty sets. Moreover, let

                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  )

                                  K 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        Q 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             be non-empty. Joins  
                               ⊔ 
                             on  
                               K 
                             are said to be  defined item-wise , if for all  
                               
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                            , whenever  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ⊔ 
                                  L 
                               
                             exists in  
                               K 
                             then  
                               
                                  (2) 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           K 
                                           ⊔ 
                                           L 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           p 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        K 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           p 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ∨ 
                                        L 
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           p 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        
                                        for all 
                                        
                                        p 
                                        ∈ 
                                        Q 
                                        . 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            Meets  
                               ⊓ 
                             on  
                               K 
                             are said to be  defined item-wise , if for all  
                               
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  L 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                            , whenever  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ⊓ 
                                  L

                               K

                                           K

                                           L

                                        K

                                           ( 
                                           p

                                        Q 
                                        .

Given any lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , whenever  
                         
                            K 
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  V

                            K

                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                       which are partially ordered by  
                         ⊑ 
                       form a natural generalization of knowledge structures on dichotomous items, where the order is given by set-inclusion. Before the notion of a polytomous knowledge structure is introduced, notice that in the dichotomous case the assumption that both the empty set  
                         0̸ 
                       (representing the naïve state) and the domain  
                         Q 
                       (representing complete mastery) are to be knowledge states in every knowledge structure has an important implication. In order to make this consequence explicit, we reformulate the dichotomous case within the present framework. The following example provides the details.

                               }

Definition 2 
                         Let  
                               Q 
                            ,  
                               V 
                             be finite non-empty sets, and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  V 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             a lattice with top and bottom elements  
                               ⊤ 
                             and  
                               ⊥ 
                            . Moreover, let  
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             denote the set of all mappings from  
                               Q 
                             to  
                               V 
                            , and let  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        Q 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            . Then  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  ⊑ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called a  polytomous (knowledge) structure on  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             whenever  
                               K 
                             contains  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ⊤ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ⊥ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  V 
                               
                             holds. The mappings  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                             are called  polytomous (knowledge) states .

Let  
                               Q 
                            ,  
                               V 
                             be finite non-empty sets, and  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  V 
                                  , 
                                  ≤ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             a lattice with top and bottom elements  
                               ⊤ 
                             and  
                               ⊥ 
                            . Moreover, let  
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             denote the set of all mappings from  
                               Q 
                             to  
                               V 
                            , and let  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ⊆ 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        Q 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            . Then  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  K 
                                  , 
                                  ⊑ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is called a  polytomous (knowledge) structure on  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                             whenever  
                               K 
                             contains  
                               
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ⊤ 
                                     }

                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ⊥ 
                                     } 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  ⋃ 
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  Q 
                                  × 
                                  V 
                               
                             holds. The mappings  
                               
                                  K 
                                  ∈ 
                                  K 
                               
                             are called  polytomous (knowledge) states .

Notice that this definition is stronger than that of  Schrepp (1997)  and  Stefanutti et al. (2020) , who consider any non-empty subset of  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                       a polytomous knowledge structure. Their conception, however, does not reduce to the classical notion of a knowledge structure when restricted to the dichotomous case, and certain item 
                         × 
                       value combinations may not be covered by the structure. Notice also that a polytomous knowledge structure according to  Definition 2  need not be a lattice. Conversely, not any collection  
                         
                            K 
                            ⊆ 
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         
                       which forms a lattice  
                         
                            ( 
                            K 
                            , 
                            ⊑ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a polytomous knowledge structure on  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                      .

                         Let

                                  (

                                  ,

                                  )
